15	
16	The second day of the tourney was overcast, with a gusty wind blowing from the west. The crowds should be less on a day like this, Dunk thought. It would have been easier for them to find a spot near the fence to see the jousting up close. Egg might have sat on the rail, while I stood behind him.
17	
18	Instead Egg would have a seat in the viewing box, dressed in silks and furs, while Dunk's view would be limited to the four walls of the tower cell where Lord Ashford's men had confined him. The chamber had a window, but it faced in the wrong direction. Even so, Dunk crammed himself into the window seat as the sun came up, and stared gloomily off across town and field and forest. They had taken his hempen sword belt, and his sword and dagger with it, and they had taken his silver as well. He hoped Egg or Raymun would remember Chestnut and Thunder.
19	
20	"Egg," he muttered low under his breath. His squire, a poor lad plucked from the streets of King's Landing. Had ever a knight been made such a fool? Dunk the lunk, thick as a castle wall and slow as an aurochs.
21	
22	He had not been permitted to speak to Egg since Lord Ashford's soldiers had scooped them all up at the puppet show. Nor Raymun, nor Tanselle, nor anyone, not even Lord Ashford himself. He wondered if he would ever see any of them again. For all he knew, they meant to keep him in this small room until he died. What did I think would happen? he asked himself bitterly. I knocked down a prince's son and kicked him in the face.
23	
24	Beneath these grey skies, the flowing finery of the highborn lords and great champions would not. seem quite so splendid as it had the day before. The sun, walled behind the clouds, would not brush their steel helms with brilliance, nor make their gold and silver chasings glitter and flash, but even so, Dunk wished he were in the crowd to watch the jousting. It would be a good day for hedge knights, for men in plain mail on unbarded horses.
25	
26	He could hear them, at least. The horns of the heralds carried well, and from time to time a roar from the crowd told him that someone had fallen, or risen, or done something especially bold. He heard faint hoofbeats too, and once in a great while the clash of swords or the snap of a lance. Dunk winced whenever he heard that last; it reminded him of the noise Tanselle's finger had made when Aerion broke it. There were other sounds too, closer at hand: footfalls in the hall outside his door, the stamp of hooves in the yard below, shouts and voices from the castle walls. Sometimes they drowned out the tourney.
27	
28	Dunk supposed that was just as well.
29	
30	"A hedge knight is the truest kind of knight, Dunk," the old man had told him, a long long time ago.
31	
32	"Other knights serve the lords who keep them, or from whom they hold their lands, but we serve where we will, for men whose causes we believe in.. Every knight swears to protect the weak and innocent, but we keep the vow best, I think." Queer how strong that memory seemed. Dunk had quite forgotten those words. And perhaps the old man had as well, toward the end.
33	
34	The morning turned to afternoon. The distant sounds of the tourney began to dwindle and die. Dusk began to seep into the cell, but Dunk still sat in the window seat, looking out on the gathering dark and trying to ignore his empty belly.
35	
36	And then he heard footsteps and a jangling of iron keys. He uncoiled and rose to his feet as the door opened. Two guards pushed in, one bearing an oil lamp. A servingman followed with a tray of food.
37	
38	Behind came Egg. "Leave the lamp and the food and go," the boy told them.
39	
40	They did as he commanded, though Dunk noticed that they left the heavy wooden door ajar. The smell of the food made him realize how ravenous he was. There was hot bread and honey, a bowl of pease porridge, a skewer of roast onions and well-charred meat. He sat by the tray, pulled apart the bread with his hands, and stuffed some into his mouth. "There's no knife," he observed. "Did they think I'd stab you, boy?"
41	
42	"They didn't tell me what they thought." Egg wore a close-fitting black wool doublet with a tucked waist and long sleeves lined with red satin. Across his chest was sewn the three-headed dragon of House Targaryen. "My uncle says I must humbly beg your forgiveness for deceiving you."
43	
44	"Your uncle," said Dunk. "That would be Prince Baelor."
45	
46	The boy looked miserable. "I never meant to lie."
47	
48	"But you did. About everything. Starting with your name. I never heard of a Prince Egg."
49	
50	"It's short for Aegon. My brother Aemon named me Egg. He's off at the Citadel now, learning to be a maester. And Daeron sometimes calls me Egg as well, and so do my sisters."
51	
52	Dunk lifted the skewer and bit into a chunk of meat. Goat, flavored with some lordly spice he'd never tasted before. Grease ran down his chin. "Aegon," he repeated. "Of course it would be Aegon. Like Aegon the Dragon. How many Aegons have been king?"
53	
54	"Four," the boy said. "Four Aegons."
55	
56	Dunk chewed, swallowed, and tore off some more bread. "Why did you do it? Was it some jape, to make a fool of the stupid hedge knight?"
57	
58	"No." The boy's eyes filled with tears, but he stood there manfully. "I was supposed to squire for Daeron. He's my oldest brother. I learned everything I had to learn to be a good squire, but Daeron isn't a very good knight. He didn't want to ride in the tourney, so after we left Summerhall he stole away from our escort, only instead of doubling back he went straight on toward Ashford, thinking they'd never look for us that way. It was him shaved my head. He knew my father would send men hunting us.
59	
60	Daeron has common hair, sort of a pale brown, nothing special, but mine is like Aerion's and my father's."
61	
62	"The blood of the dragon," Dunk said. "Silver-gold hair and purple eyes, everyone knows that." Thick as a castle wall, Dunk.
63	
64	"Yes. So Daeron shaved it off. He meant for us to hide until the tourney was over. Only then you took me for a stableboy, and . . ." He lowered his eyes. "I didn't care if Daeron fought or not, but I wanted to be somebody's squire. I'm sorry, ser. I truly am."
65	
66	Dunk looked at him thoughtfully. He knew what it was like to want something so badly that you would tell a monstrous lie just to get near it. "I thought you were like me," he said. "Might be you are. Only not the way I thought."
67	
68	"We're both from King's Landing still," the boy said hopefully.
69	
70	Dunk had to laugh. "Yes, you from the top of Aegon's Hill and me from the bottom."
71	
72	"That's not so far, ser."
73	
74	Dunk took a bite from an onion. "Do I need to call you m'lord or Your Grace or something?"
75	
76	"At court," the boy admitted, "but other times you can keep on calling me Egg if you like, Ser."
77	
78	"What will they do with me, Egg?"
79	
80	"My uncle wants to see you. After you're done eating, ser."
81	
82	Dunk shoved the platter aside, and stood. "I'm done now, then. I've already kicked one prince in the mouth, I don't mean to keep another waiting."
83	
84	Lord Ashford had turned his own chambers over to Prince Baelor for the duration of his stay, so it was to the lord's solar that Eggno, Aegon, he would have to get used to thatconducted him. Baelor sat reading by the light of beeswax candle. Dunk knelt before him. "Rise," the prince said. "Would you care for wine?"
85	
86	"As it please you, Your Grace."
87	
88	"Pour Ser Duncan a cup of the sweet Dornish red, Aegon," the prince commanded. "Try not to spill it on him, you've done him sufficient ill already."
89	
90	"The boy won't spill, Your Grace," said Dunk. "He's a good boy. A good squire. And he meant no harm to me, I know."
91	
92	"One need not intend harm to do it. Aegon should have come to me when he saw what his brother was doing to those puppeteers. Instead he ran to you. That was no kindness. What you did, ser . . . well, I might have done the same in your place, but I am a prince of the realm, not a hedge knight. It is never wise to strike a king's grandson in anger, no matter the cause."
93	
94	Dunk nodded grimly. Egg offered him a silver goblet, brimming with wine. He accepted it and took a long swallow.
95	
96	"I hate Aerion," Egg said with vehemence. "And I had to run for Ser Duncan, uncle, the castle was too far."
97	
98	"Aerion is your brother," the prince said firmly, "and the septons say we must love our brothers. Aegon, leave us now, I would speak with Ser Duncan privately."
99	
100	The boy put down the flagon of wine and bowed stiffly. "As you will, Your Grace." He went to the door of the solar and closed it softly behind him.
101	
102	Baelor Breakspear studied Dunk's eyes for a long moment. "Ser Duncan, let me ask you thishow good a knight are you, truly? How skilled at arms?"
103	
104	Dunk did not know what to say. "Ser Arlan taught me sword and shield, and how to tilt at rings and quintains."
105	
106	Prince Baelor seemed troubled by that answer. "My brother Maekar returned to the castle a few hours ago. He found his heir drunk in an inn a day's ride to the south. Maekar would never admit as much, but I believe it was his secret hope that his sons might outshine mine in this tourney. Instead they have both shamed him, but what is he to do? They are blood of his blood. Maekar is angry, and must needs have a target for his wrath. He has chosen you.
107	
108	"Me?" Dunk said miserably.
109	
110	"Aerion has already filled his father's ear. And Daeron has not helped you either. To excuse his own cowardice, he told my brother that a huge robber knight, chance met on the road, made off with Aegon. I fear you have been cast as this robber knight, ser. In Daeron's tale, he has spent all these days pursuing you hither and yon, to win back his brother."
111	
112	"But Egg will tell him the truth. Aegon, I mean.
113	
114	"Egg will tell him, I have no doubt," said Prince Baelor, "but the boy has been known to lie too, as you have good reason to recall. Which son will my brother believe? As for the matter of these puppeteers, by the time Aerion is done twisting the tale it will be high treason. The dragon is the sigil of the royal House. To portray one being slain, sawdust blood spilling from its neck . . . well, it was doubtless innocent, but it was far from wise. Aerion calls it a veiled attack on House Targaryen, an incitement to revolt. Maekar will likely agree. My brother has a prickly nature, and he has placed all his best hopes on Aerion, since Daeron has been such a grave disappointment to him." The prince took a sip of wine, then set the goblet aside. "Whatever my brother believes or fails to believe, one truth is beyond dispute. You laid hands upon the blood of the dragon. For that offense, you must be tried, and judged, and punished."
115	
116	"Punished?" Dunk did not like the sound of that.
117	
118	"Aerion would like your head, with or without teeth. He will not have it, I promise you, but I cannot deny him a trial. As my royal father is hundreds of leagues away, my brother and I must sit in judgment of you, along with Lord Ashford, whose domains these are, and Lord Tyrell of Highgarden, his liege lord. The last time a man was found guilty of striking one of royal blood, it was decreed that he should lose the offending hand."
119	
120	"My hand?" said Dunk, aghast.
121	
122	"And your foot. You kicked him too, did you not?"
123	
124	Dunk could not speak.
125	
126	"To be sure, I will urge my fellow judges to be merciful. I am the King's Hand and the heir to the throne, my word carries some weight. But so does my brother's. The risk is there."
127	
128	"I," said Dunk, "I . . . Your Grace, I . . ." They meant no treason, it was only a wooden dragon, it was never meant to be a royal prince, he wanted to say, but his words had deserted him once and all. He had never been any good with words.
129	
130	"You have another choice, though," Prince Baelor said quietly. "Whether it is a better choice or a worse one, I cannot say, but I remind you that any knight accused of a crime has the right to demand trial by combat. So I ask you once again, Ser Duncan the Tallhow good a knight are you? Truly?"
131	
132	A trial of seven," said Prince Aerion, smiling. "That is my right, I do believe.
133	
134	Prince Baelor drummed his fingers on the table, frowning. To his left, Lord Ashford nodded slowly.
135	
136	"Why?" Prince Maekar demanded, leaning forward toward his son. "Are you afraid to face this hedge knight alone, and let the gods decide the truth of your accusations?"
137	
138	"Afraid?" said Aerion. "Of such as this? Don't be absurd, Father. My thought is for my beloved brother.
139	
140	Daeron has been wronged by this Ser Duncan as well, and has first claim to his blood. A trial of seven allows both of us to face him."
141	
142	"Do me no favors, brother," muttered Daeron Targaryen. The eldest son of Prince Maekar looked even worse than he had when Dunk had encountered him in the inn. He seemed to be sober this time, his red-and-black doublet unstained by wine, but his eyes were bloodshot, and a fine sheen of sweat covered his brow. "I am content to cheer you on as you slay the rogue."
143	
144	"You are too kind, sweet brother," said Prince Aerion, all smiles, "but it would be selfish of me to deny you the right to prove the truth of your words at the hazard of your body. I must insist upon a trial of seven."
145	
146	Dunk was lost. "Your Grace, my lords," he said to the dais. "I do not understand. What is this trial of seven?"
147	
148	Prince Baelor shifted uncomfortably in his seat. "It is another form of trial by combat. Ancient, seldom invoked. It came across the narrow sea with the Andals and their seven gods. In any trial by combat, the accuser and accused are asking the gods to decide the issue between them. The Andals believed that if the seven champions fought on each side, the gods, being thus honored, would be more like to take a hand and see that a just result was achieved."
149	
150	"Or mayhap they simply had a taste for swordplay," said Lord Leo Tyrell, a cynical smile touching his lips. "Regardless, Ser Aerion is within his rights. A trial of seven it must be."
151	
152	"I must fight seven men, then?" Dunk asked hopelessly.
153	
154	"Not alone, ser," Prince Maekar said impatiently.
155	
156	"Don't play the fool, it will not serve. It must be seven against seven. You must needs find six other knights to fight beside you."
157	
158	Six knights, Dunk thought. They might as well have told him to find six thousand. He had no brothers, no cousins, no old comrades who had stood beside him in battle. Why would six strangers risk their own lives to defend a hedge knight against two royal princelings? "Your Graces, my lords," he said, "what if no one will take my part?"
159	
160	Maekar Targaryen looked down on him coldly. "If a cause is just, good men will fight for it. If you can find no champions, ser, it will be because you are guilty. Could anything be more plain?"
161	
162	Dunk had never felt so alone as he did when he walked out the gates of Ashford Castle and heard the portcullis rattle down behind him. A soft rain was falling, light as dew on his skin, and yet he shivered at the touch of it. Across the river, colored rings haloed the scant few pavilions where fires still burned.
163	
164	The night was half gone, he guessed. Dawn would be on him in a few hours. And with dawn comes death.
165	
166	They had given him back his sword and silver, yet as he waded across the ford, his thoughts were bleak.
167	
168	He wondered if they expected him to saddle a horse and flee. He could, if be wished. That would be the end of his knighthood, to be sure; he would be no more than an outlaw henceforth, until the day some lord took him and struck off his head. Better to die a knight than live like that, he told himself stubbornly. Wet to the knee, he trudged past the empty lists. Most of the pavilions were dark, their owners long asleep, but here and there a few candles still burned. Dunk heard soft moans and cries of pleasure coming from within one tent. It made him wonder whether he would die without ever having known a maid.
169	
170	Then he heard the snort of a horse, a snort he somehow knew for Thunder's. He turned his steps and ran, and there he was, tied up with Chestnut outside a round pavilion lit from within by a vague golden glow.
171	
172	On its center pole the banner hung sodden, but Dunk could still make out the dark curve of the Fossoway apple. It looked like hope.
173	
174	A trial by combat," Raymun said heavily. "Gods be good, Duncan, that means lances of war, morningstars, battle-axes . . . the swords won't be blunted, do you understand that?"
175	
176	"Raymun the Reluctant," mocked his cousin Ser Steffon. An apple made of gold and garnets fastened his cloak of yellow wool. "You need not fear, cousin, this is a knightly combat. As you are no knight, your skin is not at risk. Ser Duncan, you have one Fossoway at least. The ripe one. I saw what Aerion did to those puppeteers. I am for you."
177	
178	"And I," snapped Raymun angrily. "I only meant"
179	
180	His cousin cut him off. "Who else fights with us, Ser Duncan?"
181	
182	Dunk spread his hands hopelessly. "I know no one else. Well, except for Ser Manfred Dondarrion. He wouldn't even vouch that I was a knight, he'll never risk his life for me."
183	
184	Ser Steffon seemed little perturbed. "Then we need five more good men. Fortunately, I have more than five friends. Leo Longthorn, the Laughing Storm, Lord Caron, the Lannisters. Ser Otho Bracken . . . aye, and the Blackwoods as well, though you will never get Blackwood and Bracken on the same side of a melee. I shall go and speak with some of them."
185	
186	"They won't be happy at being woken," his cousin objected.
187	
188	"Excellent," declared Ser Steffon. "If they are angry, they'll fight all the more fiercely. You may rely on me, Ser Duncan. Cousin, if I do not return before dawn, bring my armor and see that Wrath is saddled and barded for me. I shall meet you both in the challengers' paddock." He laughed. "This will be a day long remembered, I think." When he strode from the tent, he looked almost happy.
189	
190	Not so Raymun. "Five knights," he said glumly after his cousin had gone. "Duncan, I am loath to dash your hopes, but . . ."
191	
192	"If your cousin can bring the men be speaks of . . ."
193	
194	"Leo Longthorn? The Brute of Bracken? The Laughing Storm?" Raymun stood. "He knows all of them, I have no doubt, but I would be less certain that any of them know him. Steffon sees this as a chance for glory, but it means your life. You should find your own men. I'll help. Better you have too many champions than too few." A noise outside made Raymun turn his head. "Who goes there?" he demanded, as a boy ducked through the flap, followed by a thin man in a rain-sodden black cloak.
195	
196	"Egg?" Dunk got to his feet. "What are you doing here?"
197	
198	"I'm your squire," the boy said. "You'll need someone to arm you, ser."
199	
200	"Does your lord father know you've left the castle?"
201	
202	"Gods be good, I hope not." Daeron Targaryen undid the clasp of his cloak and let it slide from his thin shoulders.
203	
204	"You? Are you mad, coming here?" Dunk pulled his knife from his sheath. "I ought to shove this through your belly."
205	
206	"Probably," Prince Daeron admitted. "Though I'd sooner you poured me a cup of wine. Look at my hands." He held one out and let them all see how it shook.
207	
208	Dunk stepped toward him, glowering. "I don't care about your hands. You lied about me."
209	
210	"I had to say something when my father demanded to know where my little brother had gotten to," the prince replied. He seated himself, ignoring Dunk and his knife. "If truth be told, I hadn't even realized Egg was gone. He wasn't at the bottom of my wine cup, and I hadn't looked anywhere else, so . . ." He sighed.
211	
212	"Ser, my father is going to join the seven accusers," Egg broke in. "I begged him not to, but he won't listen. He says it is the only way to redeem Aerion's honor, and Daeron's."
213	
214	"Not that I ever asked to have my honor redeemed," said Prince Daeron sourly. "Whoever has it can keep it, so far as I'm concerned. Still, here we are. For what it's worth, Ser Duncan, you have little to fear from me. The only thing I like less than horses are swords. Heavy things, and beastly sharp. I'll do my best to look gallant in the first charge, but after that . . . well, perhaps you could strike me a nice blow to the side of the helm. Make it ring, but not too loud, if you take my meaning. My brothers have my measure when it comes to fighting and dancing and thinking and reading books, but none of them is half my equal at lying insensible in the mud."
215	
216	Dunk could only stare at him, and wonder whether the princeling was trying to play him for a fool.
217	
218	"Why did you come?"
219	
220	"To warn you of what you face," Daeron said. "My father has commanded the Kingsguard to fight with him."
221	
222	"The Kingsguard?" said Dunk, appalled.
223	
224	"Well, the three who are here. Thank the gods Uncle Baelor left the other four at King's Landing with our royal grandfather."
225	
226	Egg supplied the names. "Ser Roland Crakehall, Ser Donnel of Duskendale, and Ser Willem Wylde."
227	
228	"They have small choice in the matter," said Daeron. "They are sworn to protect the lives of the king and royal family, and my brothers and I are blood of the dragon, gods help us."
229	
230	Dunk counted on his fingers. "That makes six. Who is the seventh man?"
231	
232	Prince Daeron shrugged. "Aerion will find someone. If need be, he will buy a champion. He has no lack of gold."
233	
234	"Who do you have?" Egg asked.
235	
236	"Raymun's cousin Ser Steffon."
237	
238	Daeron winced. "Only one?"
239	
240	"Ser Steffon has gone to some of his friends."
241	
242	"I can bring people," said Egg. "Knights. I can."
243	
244	"Egg," said Dunk, "I will be fighting your own brothers."
245	
246	"You won't hurt Daeron, though," the boy said. "He told you he'd fall down. And Aerion . . . I remember, when I was little, he used to come into my bedchamber at night and put his knife between my legs. He had too many brothers, he'd say, maybe one night he'd make me his sister, then he could marry me. He threw my cat in the well too. He says he didn't, but he always lies."
247	
248	Prince Daeron gave a weary shrug. "Egg has the truth of it. Aerion's quite the monster. He thinks he's a dragon in human form, you know. That's why he was so wroth at that puppet show. A pity he wasn't born a Fossoway, then he'd think himself an apple and we'd all be a deal safer, but there you are."
249	
250	Bending, he scooped up his fallen cloak and shook the rain from it. "I must steal back to the castle before my father wonders why I'm taking so long to sharpen my sword, but before I go, I would like a private word, Ser Duncan. Will you walk with me?"
251	
252	Dunk looked at the princeling suspiciously a moment. "As you wish, Your Grace." He sheathed his dagger. "I need to get my shield too."
253	
254	"Egg and I will look for knights," promised Raymun.
255

The second day of the tourney was overcast, with a gusty wind blowing from the west. The crowds should be less on a day like this, Dunk thought. It would have been easier for them to find a spot near the fence to see the jousting up close. Egg might have sat on the rail, while I stood behind him.

Instead Egg would have a seat in the viewing box, dressed in silks and furs, while Dunk's view would be limited to the four walls of the tower cell where Lord Ashford's men had confined him. The chamber had a window, but it faced in the wrong direction. Even so, Dunk crammed himself into the window seat as the sun came up, and stared gloomily off across town and field and forest. They had taken his hempen sword belt, and his sword and dagger with it, and they had taken his silver as well. He hoped Egg or Raymun would remember Chestnut and Thunder.

"Egg," he muttered low under his breath. His squire, a poor lad plucked from the streets of King's Landing. Had ever a knight been made such a fool? Dunk the lunk, thick as a castle wall and slow as an aurochs.

He had not been permitted to speak to Egg since Lord Ashford's soldiers had scooped them all up at the puppet show. Nor Raymun, nor Tanselle, nor anyone, not even Lord Ashford himself. He wondered if he would ever see any of them again. For all he knew, they meant to keep him in this small room until he died. What did I think would happen? he asked himself bitterly. I knocked down a prince's son and kicked him in the face.

Beneath these grey skies, the flowing finery of the highborn lords and great champions would not. seem quite so splendid as it had the day before. The sun, walled behind the clouds, would not brush their steel helms with brilliance, nor make their gold and silver chasings glitter and flash, but even so, Dunk wished he were in the crowd to watch the jousting. It would be a good day for hedge knights, for men in plain mail on unbarded horses.

He could hear them, at least. The horns of the heralds carried well, and from time to time a roar from the crowd told him that someone had fallen, or risen, or done something especially bold. He heard faint hoofbeats too, and once in a great while the clash of swords or the snap of a lance. Dunk winced whenever he heard that last; it reminded him of the noise Tanselle's finger had made when Aerion broke it. There were other sounds too, closer at hand: footfalls in the hall outside his door, the stamp of hooves in the yard below, shouts and voices from the castle walls. Sometimes they drowned out the tourney.

Dunk supposed that was just as well.

"A hedge knight is the truest kind of knight, Dunk," the old man had told him, a long long time ago.

"Other knights serve the lords who keep them, or from whom they hold their lands, but we serve where we will, for men whose causes we believe in.. Every knight swears to protect the weak and innocent, but we keep the vow best, I think." Queer how strong that memory seemed. Dunk had quite forgotten those words. And perhaps the old man had as well, toward the end.

The morning turned to afternoon. The distant sounds of the tourney began to dwindle and die. Dusk began to seep into the cell, but Dunk still sat in the window seat, looking out on the gathering dark and trying to ignore his empty belly.

And then he heard footsteps and a jangling of iron keys. He uncoiled and rose to his feet as the door opened. Two guards pushed in, one bearing an oil lamp. A servingman followed with a tray of food.

Behind came Egg. "Leave the lamp and the food and go," the boy told them.

They did as he commanded, though Dunk noticed that they left the heavy wooden door ajar. The smell of the food made him realize how ravenous he was. There was hot bread and honey, a bowl of pease porridge, a skewer of roast onions and well-charred meat. He sat by the tray, pulled apart the bread with his hands, and stuffed some into his mouth. "There's no knife," he observed. "Did they think I'd stab you, boy?"

"They didn't tell me what they thought." Egg wore a close-fitting black wool doublet with a tucked waist and long sleeves lined with red satin. Across his chest was sewn the three-headed dragon of House Targaryen. "My uncle says I must humbly beg your forgiveness for deceiving you."

"Your uncle," said Dunk. "That would be Prince Baelor."

The boy looked miserable. "I never meant to lie."

"But you did. About everything. Starting with your name. I never heard of a Prince Egg."

"It's short for Aegon. My brother Aemon named me Egg. He's off at the Citadel now, learning to be a maester. And Daeron sometimes calls me Egg as well, and so do my sisters."

Dunk lifted the skewer and bit into a chunk of meat. Goat, flavored with some lordly spice he'd never tasted before. Grease ran down his chin. "Aegon," he repeated. "Of course it would be Aegon. Like Aegon the Dragon. How many Aegons have been king?"

"Four," the boy said. "Four Aegons."

Dunk chewed, swallowed, and tore off some more bread. "Why did you do it? Was it some jape, to make a fool of the stupid hedge knight?"

"No." The boy's eyes filled with tears, but he stood there manfully. "I was supposed to squire for Daeron. He's my oldest brother. I learned everything I had to learn to be a good squire, but Daeron isn't a very good knight. He didn't want to ride in the tourney, so after we left Summerhall he stole away from our escort, only instead of doubling back he went straight on toward Ashford, thinking they'd never look for us that way. It was him shaved my head. He knew my father would send men hunting us.

Daeron has common hair, sort of a pale brown, nothing special, but mine is like Aerion's and my father's."

"The blood of the dragon," Dunk said. "Silver-gold hair and purple eyes, everyone knows that." Thick as a castle wall, Dunk.

"Yes. So Daeron shaved it off. He meant for us to hide until the tourney was over. Only then you took me for a stableboy, and . . ." He lowered his eyes. "I didn't care if Daeron fought or not, but I wanted to be somebody's squire. I'm sorry, ser. I truly am."

Dunk looked at him thoughtfully. He knew what it was like to want something so badly that you would tell a monstrous lie just to get near it. "I thought you were like me," he said. "Might be you are. Only not the way I thought."

"We're both from King's Landing still," the boy said hopefully.

Dunk had to laugh. "Yes, you from the top of Aegon's Hill and me from the bottom."

"That's not so far, ser."

Dunk took a bite from an onion. "Do I need to call you m'lord or Your Grace or something?"

"At court," the boy admitted, "but other times you can keep on calling me Egg if you like, Ser."

"What will they do with me, Egg?"

"My uncle wants to see you. After you're done eating, ser."

Dunk shoved the platter aside, and stood. "I'm done now, then. I've already kicked one prince in the mouth, I don't mean to keep another waiting."

Lord Ashford had turned his own chambers over to Prince Baelor for the duration of his stay, so it was to the lord's solar that Eggno, Aegon, he would have to get used to thatconducted him. Baelor sat reading by the light of beeswax candle. Dunk knelt before him. "Rise," the prince said. "Would you care for wine?"

"As it please you, Your Grace."

"Pour Ser Duncan a cup of the sweet Dornish red, Aegon," the prince commanded. "Try not to spill it on him, you've done him sufficient ill already."

"The boy won't spill, Your Grace," said Dunk. "He's a good boy. A good squire. And he meant no harm to me, I know."

"One need not intend harm to do it. Aegon should have come to me when he saw what his brother was doing to those puppeteers. Instead he ran to you. That was no kindness. What you did, ser . . . well, I might have done the same in your place, but I am a prince of the realm, not a hedge knight. It is never wise to strike a king's grandson in anger, no matter the cause."

Dunk nodded grimly. Egg offered him a silver goblet, brimming with wine. He accepted it and took a long swallow.

"I hate Aerion," Egg said with vehemence. "And I had to run for Ser Duncan, uncle, the castle was too far."

"Aerion is your brother," the prince said firmly, "and the septons say we must love our brothers. Aegon, leave us now, I would speak with Ser Duncan privately."

The boy put down the flagon of wine and bowed stiffly. "As you will, Your Grace." He went to the door of the solar and closed it softly behind him.

Baelor Breakspear studied Dunk's eyes for a long moment. "Ser Duncan, let me ask you thishow good a knight are you, truly? How skilled at arms?"

Dunk did not know what to say. "Ser Arlan taught me sword and shield, and how to tilt at rings and quintains."

Prince Baelor seemed troubled by that answer. "My brother Maekar returned to the castle a few hours ago. He found his heir drunk in an inn a day's ride to the south. Maekar would never admit as much, but I believe it was his secret hope that his sons might outshine mine in this tourney. Instead they have both shamed him, but what is he to do? They are blood of his blood. Maekar is angry, and must needs have a target for his wrath. He has chosen you.

"Me?" Dunk said miserably.

"Aerion has already filled his father's ear. And Daeron has not helped you either. To excuse his own cowardice, he told my brother that a huge robber knight, chance met on the road, made off with Aegon. I fear you have been cast as this robber knight, ser. In Daeron's tale, he has spent all these days pursuing you hither and yon, to win back his brother."

"But Egg will tell him the truth. Aegon, I mean.

"Egg will tell him, I have no doubt," said Prince Baelor, "but the boy has been known to lie too, as you have good reason to recall. Which son will my brother believe? As for the matter of these puppeteers, by the time Aerion is done twisting the tale it will be high treason. The dragon is the sigil of the royal House. To portray one being slain, sawdust blood spilling from its neck . . . well, it was doubtless innocent, but it was far from wise. Aerion calls it a veiled attack on House Targaryen, an incitement to revolt. Maekar will likely agree. My brother has a prickly nature, and he has placed all his best hopes on Aerion, since Daeron has been such a grave disappointment to him." The prince took a sip of wine, then set the goblet aside. "Whatever my brother believes or fails to believe, one truth is beyond dispute. You laid hands upon the blood of the dragon. For that offense, you must be tried, and judged, and punished."

"Punished?" Dunk did not like the sound of that.

"Aerion would like your head, with or without teeth. He will not have it, I promise you, but I cannot deny him a trial. As my royal father is hundreds of leagues away, my brother and I must sit in judgment of you, along with Lord Ashford, whose domains these are, and Lord Tyrell of Highgarden, his liege lord. The last time a man was found guilty of striking one of royal blood, it was decreed that he should lose the offending hand."

"My hand?" said Dunk, aghast.

"And your foot. You kicked him too, did you not?"

Dunk could not speak.

"To be sure, I will urge my fellow judges to be merciful. I am the King's Hand and the heir to the throne, my word carries some weight. But so does my brother's. The risk is there."

"I," said Dunk, "I . . . Your Grace, I . . ." They meant no treason, it was only a wooden dragon, it was never meant to be a royal prince, he wanted to say, but his words had deserted him once and all. He had never been any good with words.

"You have another choice, though," Prince Baelor said quietly. "Whether it is a better choice or a worse one, I cannot say, but I remind you that any knight accused of a crime has the right to demand trial by combat. So I ask you once again, Ser Duncan the Tallhow good a knight are you? Truly?"

A trial of seven," said Prince Aerion, smiling. "That is my right, I do believe.

Prince Baelor drummed his fingers on the table, frowning. To his left, Lord Ashford nodded slowly.

"Why?" Prince Maekar demanded, leaning forward toward his son. "Are you afraid to face this hedge knight alone, and let the gods decide the truth of your accusations?"

"Afraid?" said Aerion. "Of such as this? Don't be absurd, Father. My thought is for my beloved brother.

Daeron has been wronged by this Ser Duncan as well, and has first claim to his blood. A trial of seven allows both of us to face him."

"Do me no favors, brother," muttered Daeron Targaryen. The eldest son of Prince Maekar looked even worse than he had when Dunk had encountered him in the inn. He seemed to be sober this time, his red-and-black doublet unstained by wine, but his eyes were bloodshot, and a fine sheen of sweat covered his brow. "I am content to cheer you on as you slay the rogue."

"You are too kind, sweet brother," said Prince Aerion, all smiles, "but it would be selfish of me to deny you the right to prove the truth of your words at the hazard of your body. I must insist upon a trial of seven."

Dunk was lost. "Your Grace, my lords," he said to the dais. "I do not understand. What is this trial of seven?"

Prince Baelor shifted uncomfortably in his seat. "It is another form of trial by combat. Ancient, seldom invoked. It came across the narrow sea with the Andals and their seven gods. In any trial by combat, the accuser and accused are asking the gods to decide the issue between them. The Andals believed that if the seven champions fought on each side, the gods, being thus honored, would be more like to take a hand and see that a just result was achieved."

"Or mayhap they simply had a taste for swordplay," said Lord Leo Tyrell, a cynical smile touching his lips. "Regardless, Ser Aerion is within his rights. A trial of seven it must be."

"I must fight seven men, then?" Dunk asked hopelessly.

"Not alone, ser," Prince Maekar said impatiently.

"Don't play the fool, it will not serve. It must be seven against seven. You must needs find six other knights to fight beside you."

Six knights, Dunk thought. They might as well have told him to find six thousand. He had no brothers, no cousins, no old comrades who had stood beside him in battle. Why would six strangers risk their own lives to defend a hedge knight against two royal princelings? "Your Graces, my lords," he said, "what if no one will take my part?"

Maekar Targaryen looked down on him coldly. "If a cause is just, good men will fight for it. If you can find no champions, ser, it will be because you are guilty. Could anything be more plain?"

Dunk had never felt so alone as he did when he walked out the gates of Ashford Castle and heard the portcullis rattle down behind him. A soft rain was falling, light as dew on his skin, and yet he shivered at the touch of it. Across the river, colored rings haloed the scant few pavilions where fires still burned.

The night was half gone, he guessed. Dawn would be on him in a few hours. And with dawn comes death.

They had given him back his sword and silver, yet as he waded across the ford, his thoughts were bleak.

He wondered if they expected him to saddle a horse and flee. He could, if be wished. That would be the end of his knighthood, to be sure; he would be no more than an outlaw henceforth, until the day some lord took him and struck off his head. Better to die a knight than live like that, he told himself stubbornly. Wet to the knee, he trudged past the empty lists. Most of the pavilions were dark, their owners long asleep, but here and there a few candles still burned. Dunk heard soft moans and cries of pleasure coming from within one tent. It made him wonder whether he would die without ever having known a maid.

Then he heard the snort of a horse, a snort he somehow knew for Thunder's. He turned his steps and ran, and there he was, tied up with Chestnut outside a round pavilion lit from within by a vague golden glow.

On its center pole the banner hung sodden, but Dunk could still make out the dark curve of the Fossoway apple. It looked like hope.

A trial by combat," Raymun said heavily. "Gods be good, Duncan, that means lances of war, morningstars, battle-axes . . . the swords won't be blunted, do you understand that?"

"Raymun the Reluctant," mocked his cousin Ser Steffon. An apple made of gold and garnets fastened his cloak of yellow wool. "You need not fear, cousin, this is a knightly combat. As you are no knight, your skin is not at risk. Ser Duncan, you have one Fossoway at least. The ripe one. I saw what Aerion did to those puppeteers. I am for you."

"And I," snapped Raymun angrily. "I only meant"

His cousin cut him off. "Who else fights with us, Ser Duncan?"

Dunk spread his hands hopelessly. "I know no one else. Well, except for Ser Manfred Dondarrion. He wouldn't even vouch that I was a knight, he'll never risk his life for me."

Ser Steffon seemed little perturbed. "Then we need five more good men. Fortunately, I have more than five friends. Leo Longthorn, the Laughing Storm, Lord Caron, the Lannisters. Ser Otho Bracken . . . aye, and the Blackwoods as well, though you will never get Blackwood and Bracken on the same side of a melee. I shall go and speak with some of them."

"They won't be happy at being woken," his cousin objected.

"Excellent," declared Ser Steffon. "If they are angry, they'll fight all the more fiercely. You may rely on me, Ser Duncan. Cousin, if I do not return before dawn, bring my armor and see that Wrath is saddled and barded for me. I shall meet you both in the challengers' paddock." He laughed. "This will be a day long remembered, I think." When he strode from the tent, he looked almost happy.

Not so Raymun. "Five knights," he said glumly after his cousin had gone. "Duncan, I am loath to dash your hopes, but . . ."

"If your cousin can bring the men be speaks of . . ."

"Leo Longthorn? The Brute of Bracken? The Laughing Storm?" Raymun stood. "He knows all of them, I have no doubt, but I would be less certain that any of them know him. Steffon sees this as a chance for glory, but it means your life. You should find your own men. I'll help. Better you have too many champions than too few." A noise outside made Raymun turn his head. "Who goes there?" he demanded, as a boy ducked through the flap, followed by a thin man in a rain-sodden black cloak.

"Egg?" Dunk got to his feet. "What are you doing here?"

"I'm your squire," the boy said. "You'll need someone to arm you, ser."

"Does your lord father know you've left the castle?"

"Gods be good, I hope not." Daeron Targaryen undid the clasp of his cloak and let it slide from his thin shoulders.

"You? Are you mad, coming here?" Dunk pulled his knife from his sheath. "I ought to shove this through your belly."

"Probably," Prince Daeron admitted. "Though I'd sooner you poured me a cup of wine. Look at my hands." He held one out and let them all see how it shook.

Dunk stepped toward him, glowering. "I don't care about your hands. You lied about me."

"I had to say something when my father demanded to know where my little brother had gotten to," the prince replied. He seated himself, ignoring Dunk and his knife. "If truth be told, I hadn't even realized Egg was gone. He wasn't at the bottom of my wine cup, and I hadn't looked anywhere else, so . . ." He sighed.

"Ser, my father is going to join the seven accusers," Egg broke in. "I begged him not to, but he won't listen. He says it is the only way to redeem Aerion's honor, and Daeron's."

"Not that I ever asked to have my honor redeemed," said Prince Daeron sourly. "Whoever has it can keep it, so far as I'm concerned. Still, here we are. For what it's worth, Ser Duncan, you have little to fear from me. The only thing I like less than horses are swords. Heavy things, and beastly sharp. I'll do my best to look gallant in the first charge, but after that . . . well, perhaps you could strike me a nice blow to the side of the helm. Make it ring, but not too loud, if you take my meaning. My brothers have my measure when it comes to fighting and dancing and thinking and reading books, but none of them is half my equal at lying insensible in the mud."

Dunk could only stare at him, and wonder whether the princeling was trying to play him for a fool.

"Why did you come?"

"To warn you of what you face," Daeron said. "My father has commanded the Kingsguard to fight with him."

"The Kingsguard?" said Dunk, appalled.

"Well, the three who are here. Thank the gods Uncle Baelor left the other four at King's Landing with our royal grandfather."

Egg supplied the names. "Ser Roland Crakehall, Ser Donnel of Duskendale, and Ser Willem Wylde."

"They have small choice in the matter," said Daeron. "They are sworn to protect the lives of the king and royal family, and my brothers and I are blood of the dragon, gods help us."

Dunk counted on his fingers. "That makes six. Who is the seventh man?"

Prince Daeron shrugged. "Aerion will find someone. If need be, he will buy a champion. He has no lack of gold."

"Who do you have?" Egg asked.

"Raymun's cousin Ser Steffon."

Daeron winced. "Only one?"

"Ser Steffon has gone to some of his friends."

"I can bring people," said Egg. "Knights. I can."

"Egg," said Dunk, "I will be fighting your own brothers."

"You won't hurt Daeron, though," the boy said. "He told you he'd fall down. And Aerion . . . I remember, when I was little, he used to come into my bedchamber at night and put his knife between my legs. He had too many brothers, he'd say, maybe one night he'd make me his sister, then he could marry me. He threw my cat in the well too. He says he didn't, but he always lies."

Prince Daeron gave a weary shrug. "Egg has the truth of it. Aerion's quite the monster. He thinks he's a dragon in human form, you know. That's why he was so wroth at that puppet show. A pity he wasn't born a Fossoway, then he'd think himself an apple and we'd all be a deal safer, but there you are."

Bending, he scooped up his fallen cloak and shook the rain from it. "I must steal back to the castle before my father wonders why I'm taking so long to sharpen my sword, but before I go, I would like a private word, Ser Duncan. Will you walk with me?"

Dunk looked at the princeling suspiciously a moment. "As you wish, Your Grace." He sheathed his dagger. "I need to get my shield too."

"Egg and I will look for knights," promised Raymun.

327	## POV Character's Internal State
328	- **Emotional state:** Anxious before the day (belly "hard as a rock"); humbled listening to Egg's knowledge; sickened by the killing of Humfrey's horse; then enraged and horrified at Aerion's assault on Tanselle; braced for death when captured ("he means to kill me... there was hardly even time to be afraid").
329	- **Primary preoccupation:** Whether he can win at least the first challenge on the morrow, and which champion he could beat; he studies Valarr closely and concludes "I would have a chance against him," while recognizing a hedge knight cannot challenge a prince.
330	- **Key decisions made:** To accept Raymun's hospitality despite discomfort; to run to Tanselle's aid and beat Aerion, wrenching free of Raymun's restraining hand ("he could not listen").
331	- **Self-deception flags:** Insists aloud "I saw a knight green as summer grass lose control of his lance" though "it had looked that way to him as well" that Aerion meant to kill the horse; frames his social discomfort among lords as inability to "return" hospitality.

# THK — Dunk I

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** THK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Ashford Meadow (the tourney grounds / the lists)
- **location_secondary:** the viewing stand, merchants' row, the Fossoway pavilion, the puppeteer's stall
- **approximate_timeline:** The first day of the Ashford tourney, dawn through night; continuous single day within this unit.
- **time_markers:** "The first day of the tourney dawned bright and clear"; morning; "For the rest of the morning and well into the afternoon"; "Late in the day"; "the sun was low in the west"; "the shadows of evening crept across the meadow"; "a hundred torches were lit"; night. Retrospective: Ser Humfrey Hardyng "won a great melee at Maidenpool last year"; Ser Robyn Rhysling "lost his eye to a splinter... not five years earlier"; Dunk served at Highgarden "before you were ever born."
- **chapter_summary:** On the first day of the Ashford tourney, Dunk carries the boy Egg on his shoulders among the smallfolk to watch the jousting, in which the five maid's champions (the two Ashford brothers, Leo Tyrell, Humfrey Hardyng, and Prince Valarr) defend against a stream of challengers. Prince Aerion Brightflame deliberately drives his lance into Ser Humfrey Hardyng's horse, crushing Humfrey's leg. That evening Raymun Fossoway invites Dunk to wine in his cousin Ser Steffon's pavilion, where Dunk learns of Prince Maekar's absent sons; Egg then bursts in reporting that Aerion is assaulting the puppet-girl Tanselle. Dunk beats Aerion, is seized by Aerion's men-at-arms, and is freed only when Egg reveals himself to be Aerion's brother and Prince Maekar's son.
- **unit_part:** 4 of 7

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Bright and clear at dawn; gusty winds that snapped and tugged at the pennons; "the wind whipping Ser Robyn's hair."
- **Season indicators:** A knight described as "green as summer grass"; "the morning air."
- **Time of day:** Dawn, morning, afternoon, late day, sunset, evening/night.
- **Lighting:** Sunlight flashing golden off Baelor's clasp and coronet; a hundred torches lit along the merchant's row at evening; a brazier of coals lighting the Fossoway pavilion interior; torch used to set puppets afire.
- **Sounds:** A thousand voices screaming and shouting; forty iron-shod hooves pounding; the rending crash of wood and steel; roar of approval; steel singing on steel; the almost-human screams of the dying horse; Tanselle's scream; a sprightly air on pipes and drums; heralds booming out names; trumpet fanfares.
- **Smells:** (none stated)
- **Notable sensory details:** Dunk feeling the earth tremble beneath his feet; the goat basted with honey and herbs over a cookfire; the air in the Fossoway pavilion "pleasantly warm" from the brazier; shards of broken lances flying "like wooden knives"; bright blood gouting from the speared horse.

## Events & Actions
1. **Breakfast before the first day** — Dunk buys foodstuffs; he and Egg eat goose eggs, fried bread, and bacon; Dunk has no appetite.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: their camp
2. **Dunk carries Egg on his shoulders to watch** — Egg complains he can only see arses; Dunk sits him on his shoulders on a rise six yards from the fence.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Egg
   - Location: the meadow by the lists
3. **The five champions defend against challengers (first summons)** — Ten knights ride; all ten lances break on the first pass; a roar of approval.
   - Location: the lists
   - Outcome: all lances broken; "a splendid omen"
4. **Valarr unhorses Ser Abelar Hightower** — Valarr's lance slides off the watchtower shield into Abelar's chest; Abelar is dashed to the ground and carried off dazed.
   - Agent: Prince Valarr
   - Patient: Ser Abelar Hightower
   - Instrument: black lance
   - Location: the lists
   - Witness: Dunk
   - Outcome: Abelar defeated, carried off
5. **Leo Tyrell unhelms the Grey Lion** — Leo rips the Lord of Casterly Rock's helm off; barefaced, the lord salutes and yields.
   - Agent: Leo Tyrell
   - Patient: Lord of Casterly Rock (the Grey Lion)
   - Instrument: lance
   - Outcome: the Grey Lion yields
6. **Humfrey Hardyng beats Lord Tully** — Humfrey unhorses Tully, then beats him into surrender afoot with the sword.
   - Agent: Ser Humfrey Hardyng
   - Patient: Lord Tully
   - Outcome: Tully surrenders
7. **Tybolt Lannister defeats Androw Ashford** — After several courses Androw loses shield, seat, and match.
   - Agent: Ser Tybolt Lannister
   - Patient: Androw Ashford
   - Outcome: Androw defeated
8. **Lyonel Baratheon defeats Robert Ashford** — After breaking nine lances and fighting afoot sword-against-mace, Robert admits defeat.
   - Agent: Ser Lyonel Baratheon
   - Patient: Robert Ashford
   - Outcome: Robert defeated
9. **Valarr defeats Lord Gawen Swann** — Valarr strikes Gawen's shoulder, unhorsing him; Gawen yields ("I yield, Your Grace. Well fought.").
   - Agent: Prince Valarr
   - Patient: Lord Gawen Swann
   - Instrument: lance
   - Witness: Dunk
   - Outcome: Gawen yields
10. **Leo Tyrell defeats Ser Pearse Caron** — After trading blunted longaxe blows and losing his shield, Leo breaks Caron's axe-haft and forces his surrender.
    - Agent: Leo Tyrell
    - Patient: Ser Pearse Caron
    - Outcome: Caron kneels and surrenders
11. **Humfrey Hardyng defeats Ser Joseth Mallister** — Joseth is carried off the field unconscious.
    - Agent: Ser Humfrey Hardyng
    - Patient: Ser Joseth Mallister
    - Outcome: Joseth carried off unconscious
12. **The "Battle of Humfrey"** — Humfrey Hardyng and Humfrey Beesbury splinter a dozen lances apiece.
    - Agent: Ser Humfrey Hardyng; Ser Humfrey Beesbury
    - Location: the lists
13. **Tybolt Lannister loses helm but wins afoot** — Unhorsed by Ser Jon Penrose, Tybolt breaks his sword in the fall yet fights on with shield alone to win and stay a champion.
    - Agent: Ser Tybolt Lannister
    - Patient: Ser Jon Penrose
    - Outcome: Tybolt wins, remains champion
14. **Robyn Rhysling loses his helm and refuses to yield** — Leo Tyrell strikes off his helm; Rhysling rides bare-faced three more courses before being sent cartwheeling by a blow over the heart.
    - Agent: Leo Tyrell
    - Patient: Ser Robyn Rhysling
    - Instrument: lance
    - Witness: Dunk
    - Outcome: Rhysling finally unhorsed
15. **Aerion drives his lance into Ser Humfrey Hardyng's horse** — Aerion aims low and spears the horse above the breastbone; the lance bursts out the back of its neck; the horse crashes down, smashing the barrier, and crushes Humfrey's leg. Dunk suspects Aerion intended it; Egg insists so.
    - Agent: Prince Aerion Brightflame
    - Patient: Ser Humfrey Hardyng (leg broken in two places) / Humfrey's stallion (killed)
    - Instrument: gold-tipped striped lance
    - Location: the lists
    - Witness: Dunk; Egg
    - Outcome: horse killed; Humfrey's leg crushed
16. **A man-at-arms dispatches the dying horse** — With a pole-axe, ending the screams.
    - Agent: a man-at-arms
    - Patient: Humfrey's stallion
    - Instrument: pole-axe
    - Outcome: horse killed
17. **Lord Ashford awards Humfrey the victory and Aerion's courser** — Despite Aerion's rage; Humfrey cannot continue (leg broken in two places).
    - Agent: Lord Ashford
    - Patient: Ser Humfrey Hardyng
    - Outcome: Humfrey named victor, given the courser; will withdraw
18. **Baelor sends his maester to Humfrey; counsels keeping four champions** — Baelor tells Ashford it would not be seemly to remove Humfrey's shield and pavilion; they continue with four champions.
    - Agent: Prince Baelor
    - Patient: Ser Humfrey Hardyng
    - Instigator: Prince Baelor (advising Lord Ashford)
19. **Dunk and Egg watch a puppet show of Nymeria at the merchants' row** — Two ships, a rousing sea battle; Dunk buys ale.
    - Agent: Dunk; Egg
    - Location: merchants' row
20. **Raymun Fossoway meets Dunk and invites him for wine** — Dunk follows to the Fossoway pavilion, leaving Egg at the puppet show.
    - Agent: Raymun Fossoway
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: merchants' row → Fossoway pavilion
21. **Aerion assaults Tanselle** — At the wrecked puppeteer's stall, Aerion twists Tanselle's arm and breaks one of her fingers while she pleads; his men burn and stamp the puppets.
    - Agent: Prince Aerion Brightflame
    - Patient: Tanselle
    - Location: the puppeteer's stall, merchants' row
    - Witness: Egg (who runs to fetch Dunk); Dunk (on arriving)
    - Outcome: Tanselle's finger broken
22. **Dunk beats Aerion** — Dunk shoves aside a man-at-arms, grabs Aerion, knocks him off his feet with a fist, kicks him in the belly and mouth, loosening a tooth.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: Prince Aerion Brightflame
    - Instrument: fists and boots
    - Location: the puppeteer's stall
    - Outcome: Aerion beaten, a tooth loosened, mouth bloodied
23. **Aerion's men-at-arms seize and pin Dunk** — They swarm him, shove him down, pin his arms and legs.
    - Agent: Aerion's men-at-arms (incl. Wate, Yorkel)
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Instigator: Prince Aerion
    - Outcome: Dunk restrained
24. **Aerion orders Dunk's teeth broken and his belly opened** — He commands Wate to fetch a hammer and break out all Dunk's teeth, then cut him open.
    - Agent: Prince Aerion
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Instigator: Prince Aerion (ordering Wate)
25. **Egg reveals his identity and commands the men-at-arms to release Dunk** — Egg invokes his father and uncle, names Wate and Yorkel; the men back away, one kneels, and free Dunk.
    - Agent: Egg
    - Patient: Dunk (freed) / Wate, Yorkel (commanded)
    - Outcome: Dunk released
26. **Raymun and Steffon Fossoway arrive armed** — Raymun in mail and helm, hand on sword; Ser Steffon with bared blade and half a dozen men-at-arms bearing the red apple badge.
    - Agent: Raymun Fossoway; Ser Steffon Fossoway
    - Patient: (in Dunk's defense)
    - Location: the puppeteer's stall

Baelor summons Dunk to the solar and explains that he must be tried for laying hands on the blood of the dragon — a crime that could cost him a hand and a foot — but that as an accused knight he may demand trial by combat. At the hearing Aerion invokes his right to a trial of seven, and Maekar tells Dunk he must find six other knights to fight beside him. Leaving the castle, Dunk finds his horses at the Fossoway pavilion, where Ser Steffon pledges to fight and goes to recruit friends; Egg and Daeron then arrive, and Daeron warns that Maekar has commanded the three Kingsguard present to join the seven accusers.

## POV Character's Internal State
- **Emotional state:** Gloomy and self-mocking in the cell ("Dunk the lunk, thick as a castle wall and slow as an aurochs"); bitter ("What did I think would happen? I knocked down a prince's son and kicked him in the face"); ravenous; aghast at the threatened loss of hand and foot ("My hand?... My foot?"); "Dunk had never felt so alone as he did when he walked out the gates"; bleak and shivering in the rain; a flicker of hope at the Fossoway banner ("It looked like hope").
- **Primary preoccupation:** Finding six knights willing to fight beside him in the trial of seven before dawn — "Six knights, Dunk thought. They might as well have told him to find six thousand."
- **Key decisions made:** To choose trial by combat over judgment (inferred — the hearing that follows is a trial of seven); not to flee though he could ("Better to die a knight than live like that, he told himself stubbornly"); to accept Daeron's request to walk and talk privately.
- **Self-deception flags:** Repeatedly calls himself stupid — "Dunk the lunk, thick as a castle wall"; "Thick as a castle wall, Dunk"; "He had never been any good with words."

# THK — Dunk I

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** THK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Ashford Castle (the tower cell and Lord Ashford's solar)
- **location_secondary:** the ford / river, the empty lists, the Fossoway pavilion across the river
- **approximate_timeline:** The second day of the Ashford tourney, from sunrise through night into the pre-dawn hours; continuous within this unit, ending in the small hours before the trial at dawn.
- **time_markers:** "The second day of the tourney was overcast"; "as the sun came up"; "The morning turned to afternoon"; "The distant sounds of the tourney began to dwindle and die"; "Dusk began to seep into the cell"; "The night was half gone, he guessed"; "Dawn would be on him in a few hours"; "with dawn comes death"; "My brother Maekar returned to the castle a few hours ago"; "a day's ride to the south" (the inn where Daeron was found); "after we left Summerhall"; "all these days pursuing you"; "a long long time ago" (Ser Arlan's remembered words); Aemon "off at the Citadel now."
- **chapter_summary:** Imprisoned in a tower cell of Ashford Castle after striking Prince Aerion, Dunk is brought food by his squire Egg, who reveals that his true name is Aegon, that he is Prince Maekar's son and Prince Baelor's nephew, and explains how his brother Daeron shaved his head to hide him. Baelor summons Dunk to the solar and explains that he must be tried for laying hands on the blood of the dragon — a crime that could cost him a hand and a foot — but that as an accused knight he may demand trial by combat. At the hearing Aerion invokes his right to a trial of seven, and Maekar tells Dunk he must find six other knights to fight beside him. Leaving the castle, Dunk finds his horses at the Fossoway pavilion, where Ser Steffon pledges to fight and goes to recruit friends; Egg and Daeron then arrive, and Daeron warns that Maekar has commanded the three Kingsguard present to join the seven accusers.
- **unit_part:** 5 of 7

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Overcast with a gusty wind blowing from the west; grey skies; the sun "walled behind the clouds"; later "a soft rain was falling, light as dew on his skin."
- **Season indicators:** (none stated beyond the overcast, windy, rainy day)
- **Time of day:** Sunrise, morning, afternoon, dusk, night ("the night was half gone"), pre-dawn.
- **Lighting:** The cell window faces "the wrong direction"; guards bring an oil lamp; Baelor reads "by the light of beeswax candle"; the Fossoway pavilion is "lit from within by a vague golden glow"; a few candles still burn in scattered pavilions; "colored rings haloed the scant few pavilions where fires still burned."
- **Sounds:** Horns of the heralds; roars from the crowd; faint hoofbeats; the clash of swords and snap of a lance; footfalls in the hall; stamp of hooves in the yard; shouts and voices from the castle walls; a jangling of iron keys; the portcullis rattling down; the snort of a horse (Thunder's); "soft moans and cries of pleasure coming from within one tent."
- **Smells:** "The smell of the food made him realize how ravenous he was."
- **Notable sensory details:** Dunk cramming himself into the window seat; grease running down his chin as he eats; goat "flavored with some lordly spice he'd never tasted before"; the shiver at the touch of the rain; wading "wet to the knee" across the ford; the sodden Fossoway banner on its center pole.
